coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electron transfer kinetics via constructing co-catalytic system of cerium oxide and carbon dots enabling efficient electrosynthesis of hydrogen peroxide in neutral media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Weijie
Li, Jiacheng
Yang, Huiyu
Chen, Ziliang
Liu, Zhiwei
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: The sluggish kinetics coupled with the competition of four-electron pathway in neutral media diminishes the production efficiency of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e− ORR). To address this dilemma, this contribution proposes one electron modulation strategy to expedite the charge transfer capability by integrating carbon dots with CeO2 (CDs/CeO2) for the efficient neutral electrosynthesis of H2O2. It was found that the integration could effectively bolster both the activity and selectivity of 2e− ORR on CDs and CeO2. Remarkably, the highest 2e− selectivity could reach up to 92 % for the optimized CDs/CeO2, substantially surpassing that (77 %) of pristine CeO2, together with about 1.5 times enhancement of activity. Meanwhile, it also delivered a superior long durability at 20 mA cm−2 for 24 h and a high Faraday efficiency of 97 %. Further in-situ transient potential scanning (TPS) technique revealed that the pseudo-capacitance and charge storage capacity of CeO2 were significantly tuned by CDs during dynamic ORR process, being conducive to the reaction kinetics and the 2e− selectivity. The findings in this work not only extends the co-catalytic system based on CDs and rare earth materials for 2e− ORR, but also offers novel insights into correlation between their electron distribution dynamics and 2e− ORR performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rare earth oxide
Coupling effect
Charge transfer kinetics
Catalytic performance
Two electron oxygen reduction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220028), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52271223, 52202107, 52201269, \u202C52272043), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210735, 21KJB430043), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (0009/2022/ITP), Shenzhen Science and Technology Plan Project (Collaborative Innovation Special Project, SGDX20220530111203019), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic diagram for the synthesis of CDs/CeO2. (b) TEM image of 10-CDs/CeO2 at low magnification. (c) TEM image of 10-CDs/CeO2 at high magnification. (d) HAADF-STEM image, and (e-g) Element mapping images of 10-CDs/CeO2.

(a) The XRD patterns of CeO2 and 10-CDs/CeO2. (b) FT−IR spectra of CeO2, 10-CDs/CeO2 and CDs. (c) Raman spectra of CeO2, 10-CDs/CeO2 and CDs. (d) Ce 3d XPS spectra of CeO2 and 10-CDs/CeO2. (e) C 1s XPS spectra of CDs and 10-CDs/CeO2. (f) O 1s XPS spectra of CDs and 10-CDs/CeO2.

(a) LSV curves of CeO2, 5-CDs/CeO2, 10-CDs/CeO2, 15-CDs/CeO2 and CDs. The bottom half part represents the disk current density, while the upper half part represents the ring current. (b) The calculated H2O2 selectivity of the five samples. (c) The calculated electron transfer number of the five samples. (d) Comparison of LSV curves of 10-CDs/CeO2 after initial and 5 K ADT cycles. (e) The calculated electron transfer number of 10-CDs/CeO2 after initial and 5 K ADT cycles. (f) The chronoamperometry curve of the 10-CDs/CeO2 in 24 h. Inset in f shows the H-Cell apparatus for testing.
(a) Schematic diagram and (b) Equivalent circuit of TPS test. TPS curves of (c) CeO2 and (d) 10-CDs/CeO2 under oxygen saturation conditions.

(a–c) The current attenuation curves obtained by TPS test of different samples under different atmosphere and low potential. (d–e) The current decay curves obtained by TPS testing of different samples under different atmosphere Faraday potentials. (f) The current decay curves obtained by TPS test of CeO2 under different atmosphere Faraday potential. (g) IMAX-U curves obtained by TPS testing under different conditions. (h) C-U curves obtained by TPS testing under different conditions. (i) Q-U curves obtained by TPS testing under different conditions.
TPS curves of CeO2 for different atmospheres at (a) −0.37 V vs. Hg/HgCl2, (b) −0.46 V vs. Hg/HgCl2, (c) −0.56 V vs. Hg/HgCl2 and (d) −0.60 V vs. Hg/HgCl2 voltages.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electron transfer kinetics via constructing co-catalytic system of cerium oxide and carbon dots enabling efficient electrosynthesis of hydrogen peroxide in neutral media

Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE), MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren’ai Road, Suzhou 215123, China (Z. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This contribution proposes one electron modulation strategy to expedite the charge transfer capability of CeO2 by integrating carbon dots for the efficient neutral electrosynthesis of H2O2. Furthermore, the pseudo-capacitance and charge storage capacity of CeO2 were significantly tuned by CDs during dynamic ORR process, being conducive to the reaction kinetics and the 2e− selectivity.

The sluggish kinetics coupled with the competition of four-electron pathway in neutral media diminishes the production efficiency of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e− ORR). To address this dilemma, this contribution proposes one electron modulation strategy to expedite the charge transfer capability by integrating carbon dots with CeO2 (CDs/CeO2) for the efficient neutral electrosynthesis of H2O2. It was found that the integration could effectively bolster both the activity and selectivity of 2e− ORR on CDs and CeO2. Remarkably, the highest 2e− selectivity could reach up to 92 % for the optimized CDs/CeO2, substantially surpassing that (77 %) of pristine CeO2, together with about 1.5 times enhancement of activity. Meanwhile, it also delivered a superior long durability at 20 mA cm−2 for 24 h and a high Faraday efficiency of 97 %. Further in-situ transient potential scanning (TPS) technique revealed that the pseudo-capacitance and charge storage capacity of CeO2 were significantly tuned by CDs during dynamic ORR process, being conducive to the reaction kinetics and the 2e− selectivity. The findings in this work not only extends the co-catalytic system based on CDs and rare earth materials for 2e− ORR, but also offers novel insights into correlation between their electron distribution dynamics and 2e− ORR performance.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most important chemicals in the world, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been widely deployed as an eco-sustainable oxidant in several key areas including printing, medicine, environmental remediation and energy conversion [1–3]. Currently, the anthraquinone auto-oxidation process remains a prevalent method, but which is suffering from the high energy consumption, complex production process, inevitable by-products and large amounts of chemical waste [4–6]. Within this context, extensive researches are ongoing to develop more sustainable and cost-effective methods for H2O2 production. The electrochemical synthesis of H2O2 via the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e− ORR) is green and convenient, offering reduced energy demands and environmental footprints [7–9]. Meanwhile, it is an available pathway to achieve customer-oriented on-site synthesis of H2O2, which has been identified as a promising alternative to the energy-consuming anthraquinone process [10–12]. The 2e− ORR can be carried out in acidic, alkaline or neutral media [13]. Particularly, neutral environment is more attractive because of its environmentally friendly properties, by which corrosion can be greatly alleviated, and the potential cost of the electrolyzer can be reduced [14–16]. However, under neutral conditions, the ion diffusion in the electrolyte is much weaker, leading to the poor electron transfer ability and thus the sluggish kinetics [17–19]. In addition, the electrochemical synthesis of H2O2 is competed by the four-electron pathway during oxygen reduction reaction (4e− ORR), resulting in the lower efficiency for producing H2O2 [20,21]. Therefore, it is of great significance to search a highly selective, active and durable electrocatalyst for the electrochemical synthesis of H2O2.

Over the past decade, rare earth metal oxides have received extensive attention as co-catalysts to improve the electrocatalytic performance of transition metal active components due to their excellent corrosion resistance, abundant oxygen vacancy, and high oxygen storage capacity [22,23]. Recently, as a pioneering work, Hota et al. found that several rare earth oxides have good intrinsic catalytic activity towards alkaline 2e− ORR. Typically, Nd2O3 mixed with carbon ink showed 91.91 % selectivity for the H2O2 production [24]. Following this observation, Chang et al. recently reported a CeO2 nanoarchitecture, The CeO2 nanoarchitecture delivers 83 % H2O2 selectivity and the H2O2 yield reaches 632 mmol gcat −1h−1 after 4 h continuous 2e− ORR reaction at 0.5 V, and the faradaic efficiency could reach up to 95.3 % in 0.1 M KOH [25]. Obviously, these investigations are encouraging, offering the chance for exploring the rare earth metal oxides as advanced 2e− ORR electrocatalysts. Nevertheless, the electrocatalysts presented in these studies are confined to alkaline environment, and there remains a lack of literature regarding rare earth oxide-based electrocatalysts for applications in neutral pH conditions. Moreover, how to improve the reaction kinetics of rare earth oxides for 2e− ORR in neutral media is still a formidable challenge.

On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of material with the diameter of less than 10 nm, with rich functional groups (–COOH, –OH, –CO, etc.), and has broad application prospects in luminescence, biology, catalysis and other fields [26]. As a co-catalyst, CDs can exert a significant impact on the electron transfer of coupling system [27,28]. In prior research, Song et al. demonstrated that the modification of MoP with nitrogen-doped CDs could effectively enhance the charge transfer ability, leading to the enhanced HER catalytic activity close to that of commercial Pt/C catalysts (32 mV), and corroborating the efficiency of employing CDs as one subunit to modulate electrocatalytic property of system [29]. Inspired by this finding, Wu et al. improved the 2e− ORR catalytic activity of In2O3 by using CDs as a co-catalyst to regulate the electron transport kinetics of In2O3. The H2O2 selectivity of optimal In2O3/CDs-10 is close to 100 %, which is much higher than that of pure In2O3 (72 %) in 0.1 M KOH [30]. Motivated by these appealing observations, one can envision that if the rare earth oxides and CDs can be combined, a favorable electron transfer capability can be gained for the system, and thus enhanced electrocatalytic 2e− ORR performance in neutral media. Furthermore, although the electron transfer kinetics has been considered to be one of the most important factors affecting the selectivity of oxygen reduction [31], to our knowledge, the charge distribution dynamics during 2e− ORR for catalytic system, has never been pursued.

Bear the above-mentioned points in mind, in this work, we synthesized CDs/CeO2 composites to study their 2e− ORR performance in neutral media and investigate the corresponding electron distribution dynamics. As expected, the H2O2 selectivity for the optimal composite (10-CeO2/CDs) can reach up to as high as 92 %, which is much higher than that of CeO2 (77 %). Moreover, the limited current density achieved by the 10-CeO2/CDs composite is approximately 1.5 times greater than that of pristine CeO2, signifying a substantial enhancement in the activity of CeO2 facilitated by CDs. Concurrently, the H2O2 generation efficiency for the 10-CeO2/CDs system reaches 97 %, in conjunction with an impressive durability sustained over a 24-hour period. Additionally, we employed a home-made transient potential scanning (TPS) technique to analyze the differences in electron distribution dynamics between CeO2 and the 10-CeO2/CDs composite. Our findings elucidate that CDs can significantly modulate the electron distribution within CeO2 by altering its pseudo-capacitance, charge transfer dynamics, and oxygen adsorption capabilities. This feasible tuning leads to a 10-CeO2/CDs catalyst that displays increased selectivity and activity. Thus, our research highlights the strategic manipulation of charge transfer as an effective approach to enhance the catalytic performance. Moreover, it confirms that analyzing the dynamics of electron distribution in catalytic systems is a viable method for unraveling complex mechanisms in intricate environments, guiding the development of highly efficient electrocatalysts.

Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 98.0 %), ethanol (99.5 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, > 95 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). These materials were used in this study without further purification. Nafion perfluorinated resin solution (5 wt%) was purchased from Adamas-Betas; Anion exchange membrane was purchased from Fumasep. Milli-Q ultrapure water (Millipore, ≥ 18.25 MΩ cm−1) was used throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, CeO2 and CDs/CeO2
ce:section.ce:para: CDs was prepared using graphite rod as carbon source by electrolysis method. DC regulated power supply with a fixed voltage of 30 V was used as the power supply, and pure water was used as the electrolyte for electrolysis. After 60 days of electrolysis, the black solution was collected and filtered to remove large carbon particles. Finally, the black solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs.

For the synthesis of CeO2, 0.88 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O was added to 15 mL 6 M NaOH solution by ultrasound until completely dissolved. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 180 °C for 24 h, cooled to room temperature, and the product was collected, washed with H2O/ethanol several times, and dried and ground to obtain CeO2 powder. 0.1 g CeO2 powder was added into 0 mg CDs, 5 mg CDs, 10 mg CDs and 15 mg CDs respectively, and dispersed in 5 mL pure water, stirring overnight until evenly mixed. Under N2 protection, samples with different mixing ratios of CDs were calcined in a tube furnace at 300 °C for 2 h to obtain CeO2, 5-CDs/CeO2, 10-CDs/CeO2, 15-CDs/CeO2.

H2O2 selectivity and number of transferred electrons (n) were obtained via rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurement. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out on CHI760E electrochemical station (Chenghua, Shanghai). A standard three-electrode setup was used with the RRDE loaded with catalyst as the working electrode, carbon rod as the counter electrode, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The ORR test was performed in an O2 saturated electrolyte (0.1 mol/L K2SO4). Catalyst ink was prepared by ultrasonic mixing of a mixture of 3.6 mg catalyst, 1 mL of 0.5 wt.% Nafion solution and 0.4 mg carbon black. Then, 6.2 μL of catalyst ink was poured onto the RRDE electrode and dried at room temperature to obtain a catalyst loading of 0.2 mg cm−2. Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was used to investigate the ORR activity and selectivity of different catalysts at 0.1 M K2SO4 and 1600 rpm.H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer number were calculated as follows:
n
=
4
I D
I D
+
I R
/ N
H 2
O 2
( % )
= 200
I R
/ N
I D
+
I R
/ N
where IR represents the ring current, ID represents the absolute value of disk current and the collection efficiency (N) of the RRDE is determined as 0.37 [32].

Electrocatalytic H2O2 production on hydrophobic carbon paper loaded with catalysts (0.05 mg cm−2) was performed in a H-Cell filled with 40 mL electrolyte (0.1 M K2SO4). In this setup, the working electrode is the carbon paper of the supported catalyst, with platinum foil as the reverse electrode. The catalyst ink was prepared by mixing 0.5 mg catalyst with 0.1 mL 5 % nafion, 0.6 mL H2O and 0.3 mL ethanol. The reaction was evaluated by chronopotentiometry with a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) at a constant potential of 0 V for testing the 1800 s without ohmic drop correction. The yield of H2O2 was determined by potassium permanganate (KMnO4) titration.

1 mL electrolyte was extracted, and the generated H2O2 concentration was quantified by titration with KMnO4 solution (0.02 mol/L). H2O2 productivity was determined by its reaction with MnO4 −:
2MnO4 − +5H2O2 + 6H+→2Mn2++5O2 + 8H2O

The faraday efficiency (FE) and yield (YR) of H2O2 generation of the H-Cell were calculated as follows:
FE =
2 ∗ 96500 ∗
M
H 2
O 2
C
,
YR =
M
H 2
O 2
g
cat
∗ t
, where MH2O2 is the number of moles of H2O2 generation, C is the total charge consumed, t is the electrolytic time, and gcat is the catalyst mass.

In order to verify the accuracy of the determination of hydrogen peroxide concentration, eFOX solution standard colorimetry was used to determine the concentration of H2O2 during the catalysis process and the results were compared with potassium permanganate titration. The preparation method for eFOX solution: Fe(NH4)2(SO4)2·6H2O (98.05 mg), d-sorbitol (18.20 mg), and xylenol orange (71.65 mg) were dissolved in a mixture of ultrapure H2O (1000 mL), ethanol (10 mL), and H2SO4 (98 %, 1.36 mL). Subsequently, 1 mL the cathode solution obtained after reaction at 0 V vs. RHE voltage for 1800 s in H-Cell was mixed with 10 mL the prepared eFOX solution and then stood for 1 h. The characteristic absorption spectrum of the mixture at 554 nm was monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy, and the concentration of H2O2 was quantitatively determined. The calibration curve was obtained in the previous work:
A = 0.000414603 ∗
C 1
+ 0.00327
[40], A of absorbance for solution, C1 for H2O2 concentration in the solution. H2O2 concentration in the reaction liquid of
C 2
=
0.011 ∗
C 1
0.001
, H2O2 yield of catalysts for
YR =
C 2
∗ V
m ∗ t
.

To investigate the kinetics of charge distribution on the catalyst surface, a self-designed TPS device was employed. The working electrode (glass-carbon electrode) was connected to the positive electrode, while the reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode) and counter electrode (carbon rod) were connected to the negative electrode. The fixed capacitor could be charged at different voltages, and upon full charge, it is connected to the test circuit system, allowing the collection of current signals to analyze charge transfer at the catalyst interface by adjusting the voltage.

Fig. 1 a schematically illustrated the preparation of CDs/CeO2. Firstly, CeO2 nanocubes were obtained by one-pot hydrothermal method using Ce(NO3)3 as Ce source in NaOH solution (Fig. S1). Then, CeO2 and CDs were mixed evenly by stirring followed by annealing to obtain CDs/CeO2 composites. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the 10-CDs/CeO2 composites is shown in Fig. 1b, from which these composites are in the shape of nanocubes. Being well consistent with the TEM result, FESEM images of 10-CDs/CeO2 also show the cubic morphology. Note that CeO2, 5-CDs/CeO2, and 15-CDs/CeO2 all show the similar morphologies to those of 10-CDs/CeO2 (Fig. S2). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the 10-CDs/CeO2 composite displays a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in the marginal region (Fig. 1c), which can be assigned to the (002) plane of CDs. Moreover, a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm is also observed for the nanocube near to the CD, which can be assigned to the (111) plane of CeO2. These results well demonstrate the coupling between CDs and CeO2. HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 1d) and corresponding elemental mapping results (Fig. 1e-g) also confirm the presence of C, Ce, and O within the 10-CDs/CeO2 composite.

On the other hand, the XRD patterns of CeO2 and 10-CDs/CeO2 are shown in Fig. 2 a. Obviously, the peaks at 28.5°, 33.1°, 47.5°, 56.3°, 69.4°, 76.7°, 79.1° and 88.4° can be identified, which are well matched with those of the standard card JCPDS No. 04-0593 of CeO2 phase. The XRD pattern of 10-CDs/CeO2 shows the very similar peaks at the corresponding position. Note that no diffraction peaks belonging to carbon can be found, which may be due to the poor crystallization of CDs and the overlap of diffraction peaks belonging to the CeO2 (111) plane at 28.5° and the carbon (002) plane at around 28°. Similarly, other CDs/CeO2 composites show the similar XRD patterns (Fig. S3). Nevertheless, the signals for CDs can be extracted from Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Specifically, Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectra of CeO2, 10-CDs/CeO2, and CDs. Compared with CeO2, several significant new absorption peaks were observed at 1720, 1450, and 1388 cm−1 for 10-CDs/CeO2, which can be identified as CO, CO, and CH bonds for stretching vibrations, respectively [33]. Similar properties have been found for other CDs/CeO2 composites (Fig. S4). These observations indicate that there are abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs, which can be considered to be conducive to tuning catalyst surfaces and improving catalytic performance. Fig. 2c shows the Raman spectra of CeO2 and 10-CDs/CeO2. Compared with CeO2, two peaks of 10-CDs/CeO2 can be observed at 1320 and 1585 cm−1, which belong to the D and G bands of CDs [34]. This proves again the synthesis 10-CDs/CeO2 composites.

The detailed elemental information of the sample surface was checked by XPS. Fig. 2d shows the 3d nuclear level peak of Ce in CeO2 and 10-CDs/CeO2, where Ce 3d spectra of CeO2 can be divided into 7 groups. Due to its highly non-stoichiometric properties, two valence states Ce3+ and Ce4+ exist in CeO2. The main peak binding energies of Ce4+ 3d3/2 and Ce4+ 3d5/2 were 917.1 and 898.7 eV, respectively. Ce3+ 3d3/2 and Ce3+ 3d5/2 have binding energies of 901.3 and 882.8 eV, respectively. The other three satellite lines SU1 and SU2, which means “concussion”, show 908.4 eV and 885.0 eV and 889.6 eV respectively on Ce3+ 3d3/2. These spectra are fully consistent with those previously reported, confirming the coexistence of Ce4+ and Ce3+ [35]. In addition, from the Ce three-dimensional nuclear level peak in 10-CDs/CeO2, it can be observed that the ratio of the integral area of Ce3+ 3d3/2 and Ce3+ 3d5/2 main peaks to the integral area of Ce4+ 3d3/2 and Ce4+ 3d5/2 main peaks is 0.8412, which is smaller than that of CeO2 1.5. This means that the presence of CeO2 contributes to the transform of Ce3+ to Ce4+. Fig. 2e shows the C 1s spectra of CDs and 10-CDs/CeO2, in which three peaks of CC/CC, CO and CO can be observed at 284.6, 285.7 and 289.1 eV in the C 1s spectra of CDs, which are in well agreement with the FT-IR results [36]. The C 1s spectra of 10-CDs/CeO2 shift to the lower energy level, which is related to the change of Ce valence state in CeO2. As shown in Fig. 2f, the main XPS peak of O 1s is superimposed near 529.7 eV. The adsorbed oxygen in CeO2 has a peak value of 531 eV [37]. The O 1s peak at 532.5 eV is independent of the presence of Ce3+ or Ce4+. It has been suggested that the peak of 532.5 eV is caused by the OH on the surface [38]. Similarly, the XPS results of 5-CDs/CeO2 and 15-CDs/CeO2 were also obtained (Figs. S5, S6), which were consistent with those results for 10-CDs/CeO2.

The selectivity of H2O2 was evaluated with a rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) in a typical three-electrode system, and the electrochemical properties of 2e− ORR were determined. All tests were performed in 0.1 M K2SO4 solution saturated with O2. The linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) curves of five prepared samples are shown in Fig. 3 a. The upper part of the LSV curve is annular current, and the lower part is disk current density. Notably, 10-CDs/CeO2 had the highest ring current among the five samples, indicating that more H2O2 was generated at 10-CDs/CeO2. Selectivity and electron transfer numbers for H2O2 were also calculated and plotted in Fig. 3b, c. 10-CDs/CeO2 has the highest H2O2 selectivity at 0.18 V vs RHE, with 92.6 % H2O2 selectivity. The results show that the ORR catalyzed by 10-CDs/CeO2 is almost dominated by 2e− pathway. At the same time, the selectivity of 15-CDs/CeO2 to H2O2 is slightly lower, about 89.6 %. The selectivity of CDs, CeO2 and 5-CDs/CeO2 for H2O2 was 62.9 %, 77.4 % and 90.6 %, respectively. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 3c, the electron transfer number of 10-CDs/CeO2 is around 2.14, demonstrating again the dominance of the 2e− ORR process. The electron transfer numbers of CDs, CeO2, 5-CDs/CeO2 and 15-CDs/CeO2 are 2.75, 2.45, 2.19 and 2.20, respectively. Note that by comparing the LSV curves of CDs under N2 and O2 conditions, it can be seen that CDs bear a certain intrinsic activity and selectivity towards 2e− ORR (Fig. S7). Upon coupling it with CeO2, both of these two subunits displayed enhanced 2e− ORR activity and selectivity, suggesting their co-catalytic feature. The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) of different samples were measured by double-layer capacitance (Figs. S8 and S9). With the increase of CDs content, the fitting slope increases, indicating that CDs can act as a co-catalyst and regulate the number of available active site of the catalyst. Importantly, the 2e− ORR performance of 10-CDs/CeO2 under neutral conditions was also compared with other reported literature, and its performance exceeded most previously documented catalysts (Table S1).

An accelerated durability test (ADT) was performed in O2-saturated 0.1 M K2SO4 solution to evaluate the durability of 10-CDs/CeO2. After 5 K ADT cycles, the weak current attenuation is shown in Fig. 3d, and the corresponding H2O2 selectivity (Fig. S10) and electron transfer number (Fig. 3e) are calculated. As was observed, 10-CDs/CeO2 has excellent stability. The chronoamperometry method is also used to evaluate the long-term stability of 10-CDs/CeO2, as shown in Fig. 3f. Even after 24 h of testing, the selectivity and activity degrade a little. The morphology of 10-CDs/CeO2 after chrono-current test was observed by TEM, and the morphology was almost unchanged (Fig. S11). In addition, there is basically no shift in the XPS spectra of 10-CDs/CeO2 after chrono-current testing, which proves the excellent stability of the catalyst (Fig. S12). These results suggest that 10-CDs/CeO2 is a potential catalyst for H2O2 production in neutral media.

Although 10-CDs/CeO2 had been shown to be a promising catalyst for H2O2 production, further testing of its capability to generate H2O2 on a large scale is still needed. Here we used H-Cell for testing to prove its good practicability. The 10-CDs/CeO2 catalyst was loaded on carbon paper as a cathode and then assembled in an H-Cell filled with 0.1 M K2SO4 saturated with O2 for H2O2 yield test. Chronoamperometry method (CA) was performed at a fixed potential RHE for 1800 s. The H2O2 yield was determined by potassium permanganate titration. The H2O2 yield of 10-CDs/CeO2 was as high as 1000 mmol g−1 cat h−1. In addition, it achieved an excellent FE of about 97 %. In order to verify the accuracy of the determination of H2O2 concentration, eFOX solution standard colorimetry was used to determine the concentration of H2O2 during the catalysis process and the results were compared with potassium permanganate titration. Fig. S13 shows the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the mixed solution. It can be seen that the absorbance at 554 nm is 0.046569. H2O2 yield of catalysts is 920 mmol g−1 cat h−1. The above consistent results indicate that 10-CDs/CeO2 holds practical application prospect in neutral electrosynthesis of H2O2.

To study the effects of CDs on electron transfer kinetics, an in-situ transient potential scanning (TPS) test was employed to analyze the kinetics of charge distribution during the dynamic reaction using a homemade device (Fig. 4 a), which records sub-millisecond charging currents and their corresponding voltages. The schematic representation of the equivalent circuit for the TPS test is depicted in Fig. 4b. Capacitors C0, C2, and C3 represent the external test capacitance, the catalyst surface state capacitance along with its pseudo-capacitance, and the interfacial double-layer capacitance, respectively. It should be noted that the solid space electric region capacitance of the catalyst C1 is not considered here. Resistors R1, R2, and R3 correspond to the electrode impedance, the faraday resistance associated with the catalytic reaction, and the interfacial resistance between the catalyst and the solution, respectively. The designated U0 represents the initial charging potential applied to the test capacitor C0. Once charged, the capacitor C0 is connected to the catalytic system, with its positive terminal linked to the working electrode and the negative terminal to the counter electrode, enabling the measurement of the electrical current within the circuit. The TPS curves of the sample are shown in Fig. 4c, d. The voltage displayed on the i-t curve corresponds to the set voltage maintained throughout the TPS test. All profiles exhibit an initial decay from their maximum values, subsequently diminishing until equilibrium is reached.

To elucidate the influence of CDs on the 2e− ORR process catalyzed by CeO2, we integrated TPS findings with reaction mechanisms to discern their interactions. The TPS experiments were conducted under non-Faraday conditions for pristine CeO2, CDs, and 10-CDs/CeO2, at holding potentials of −0.11 V, −0.11 V, and −0.31 V vs. Hg/HgCl2, respectively. The actual potentials, as marked in the current–time (I-T) curves, were determined from the potential-time (U-T) curves obtained during TPS, which are illustrated in Fig. S15–21. By fitting the initial segment of the decay curve on a microsecond timescale, we derived the decay constant (τ), which reflects the rate of charge dissipation within the system, as depicted in Fig. 5 a-c. This approach ensures that the analysis of τ is isolated from the effects of prolonged reactions and solely represents the initial stages of charge decay. Under N2-saturated environment, the τ for CeO2, CDs, and 10-CDs/CeO2 were recorded as 0.000695, 0.000319, and 0.01499 s, respectively. In contrast, under O2-saturated conditions, these values shifted to 0.00186 s for CeO2, 0.00281 s for CDs, and 0.00309 s for 10-CDs/CeO2. Notably, the increased τ values under O2 saturation indicate a slower charge decay rate due to the adsorption of oxygen, which may influence the formation of an electric double layer at the interface between the catalyst and the electrolyte. Conversely, under N2 saturation, a rapid establishment of the interfacial double layer facilitates prompt electrical neutrality within the system. Furthermore, the significantly higher τ value for CDs compared to CeO2 suggests that the charge transfer kinetics in the CDs/CeO2 composite are predominantly governed by the CDs. This observation underscores the substantial role of CDs in modulating the electrochemical behavior of the composite material.

Furthermore, the TPS test at Faraday potential obtained the current–time curves of different samples under different atmospheres at different potentials (Fig. 5d, e and S22). Under the condition of N2 saturation, it can be seen that the current of the composite CDs/CeO2 is significantly stronger than that of CeO2, and can maintain a high current state for a long time. However, the impedance of the composite CDs/CeO2 is higher than that of CeO2 (Fig. S23), which means that the charge storage capacity of the catalyst is the main factor determining the current intensity during the TPS test of the sample compared with the impedance. Therefore, we can judge that the composite CDs/CeO2 has a stronger charge storage capacity (Fig. 5i). The negative correlation between large impedance and strong current shown by 10-CDs/CeO2 should be attributed to the fact that its stronger charge storage capacity will redistribute the charge in the test system, and more charge will be transferred from the charging capacitor to the test system, thus exhibiting a larger current. In particular, the stronger charge storage capacity of 10-CDs/CeO2 is favorable for delayed charge transfer, and its slower charge transfer kinetics is favorable for 2e− ORR. Under the condition of O2 saturation, we can see that the current of the composite CDs/CeO2 is still significantly stronger than that of CeO2, and the high current state is maintained for a longer time, which shows that the oxygen participating in the ORR reaction consumes the stored charge of the catalyst, so the charging capacitor needs to donate more charges to make the circuit current greater. According to the size of charge storage peak, the ORR reactivity of CDs/CeO2 composites is obviously better than that of CeO2 [39]. In addition, it can be observed that the maximum charging current (IMAX) under N2 condition is larger than that of under O2 condition. This can be due to the increased impedance of the catalyst when exposed to an oxygen environment from the perspective of inherent electrical characteristics of a resistor–capacitor (RC) circuit (Fig. S23).

Comparison of the current–time profiles for CeO2 under varying atmospheric conditions reveals that CeO2 exhibits consistent behavior irrespective of the atmosphere, showing analogous characteristics in both N2 and O2 environments (Fig. 5f, S25). This suggests that the responsivity of the CDs/CeO2 composite to oxygen is significantly influenced by CDs. To elucidate the impact of CDs on the 2e− ORR, TPS measurements were conducted on CDs across different atmospheric conditions (Fig. S26). It was observed that the current response of CDs was amplified under an O2-saturated atmosphere, attributed to an increase in sample capacitance due to oxygen adsorption onto the CDs (Fig. S27c). Conversely, over extended periods, the CDs exhibited a charge storage peak under an N2 atmosphere, indicative of the formation of an interfacial double layer that enhances the charge storage capacity of the CDs. Under O2 atmosphere, the CDs exhibited a rapid decay over time. This behavior is likely due to the influence of an electric double layer at the interface by oxygen adsorption, leading to a swift decline in current. Furthermore, even though the impedance of CDs was greater in the presence of O2 compared to N2 (as shown in Fig. S24), a higher IMAX value was still noted in the O2 environment than in the N2 environment. This discrepancy could be explained by a more substantial contribution from pseudo-capacitance in the case of CDs under oxygen conditions.

To thoroughly analyze the reaction dynamics of the composite CDs/CeO2 over an extended period and the influence of CDs on CeO2, we further compared the current–time curves of the composite under various atmospheric conditions and voltages (Fig. 6 ). Initially, it was observed that the current is higher under N2 atmosphere due to the larger capacitance of the composite CDs/CeO2 in the absence of oxygen (Fig. 5g, h). With time increasing, the current–time curves for the composite exhibit an intersection point. This intersection occurs because the oxygen participating in the ORR reaction depletes the charge of the composite CDs/CeO2, consequently boosting the charging current. At this intersection, the rate at which charge is received, that is, the formation rate of the double electric layer at the catalyst interface aligns with the rate of charge consumption during the oxygen reaction. Once the charging potential stabilizes, the stable current reflects the rate at which the catalyst externally releases charge. By comparing the stable currents under different atmospheres and voltages across various samples, we note that the stable current is greater under O2 atmosphere (Fig. S28), which is indicative of more charge being consumed by the ORR reaction. Furthermore, the stable current of CDs escalates more rapidly with voltage (Fig. S27d), suggesting that CDs facilitate a faster electron transfer within the composite CDs/CeO2.

In our study, we developed the co-electrocatalysts based on CDs and CeO2 for efficient 2e− ORR in neutral media. The designed CDs/CeO2 could exhibit the highest 2e− selectivity of 92 %, substantially surpassing that of pristine CeO2 (77 %), together with about 1.5 times enhancement of activity. Meanwhile, it also gained a high faraday efficiency of 97 % and a long durability at 20 mA cm−2 for 24 h. To better understand how the movement of electrons affects the process of the catalytic process of CDs/CeO2, we used TPS technology as one powerful kinetic analysis tool to show that CDs could effectively control how charges were distributed in the reaction system, including the effects of oxygen binding to the catalyst, the ability of one catalyst to store electrons, and how quickly electrons move. Appropriate incorporation of CDs could greatly contribute to the reaction kinetics and thermodynamics, thus the enhancement of the selectivity and activity of CeO2. This work is expected to offer fresh ideas for creating more efficient catalysts in the future.

Weijie Yuan: Investigation. Jiacheng Li: Data curation. Huiyu Yang: Methodology. Ziliang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Zhiwei Liu: Investigation. Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Yang Liu: Writing – original draft, Resources. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

Shao, 1D Monoclinic IrxRu1‐xO2 solid solution with Ru‐enhanced electrocatalytic activity for acidic oxygen evolution reaction, Adv. Funct. Mater. (2024) 2402226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient photo-driven generation and elimination of reactive oxygen species in biological environment by gold nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.N. Li, T.Y. Zhang, J. Shen, Y.R. Ma, K. Yu, J.C. Li, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z.H. Kang, Highly efficient photo-driven generation and elimination of reactive oxygen species in biological environment by gold nanoclusters, J. Environ. Chem. Eng., 11 (2023) 111546.
